records.ety: [OE. brik, F. brique; of Ger. origin; cf. AS. brice a breaking, fragment, Prov. E. brique piece, brique de pain, equiv. to AS. hlāfes brice, fr. the root of E. break. See Break.]

defs: A block or clay tempered with water, sand, etc., molded into a regular form, usually rectangular, and sun-dried, or burnt in a kiln, or in a heap or stack called a clamp.
quotes: The Assyrians appear to have made much less use of bricks baked in the furnace than the Babylonians.

defs: Bricks, collectively, as designating that kind of material; as, a load of brick; a thousand of brick.
quotes: Some of Palladio's finest examples are of brick.

defs: Any oblong rectangular mass; as, a brick of maple sugar; a penny brick (of bread).
A good fellow; a merry person; as, you 're a brick.
notes: ☞ Brick is used adjectively or in combination; as, brick wall; brick clay; brick color; brick red.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Bricked (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Bricking.]

defs: To lay or pave with bricks; to surround, line, or construct with bricks.
To imitate or counterfeit a brick wall on, as by smearing plaster with red ocher, making the joints with an edge tool, and pointing them.
